data.title: TSLA, BYD Face Memory Chip Shortage in 2026, Wells Fargo Warns
data.description:
Analysts at Wells Fargo, one of Wall Street's leading research firms, released a note stating that automakers could face renewed cost pressures and potential supply interruptions as a widening global memory-chip shortage intensifies, spurred by surging demand from data centers and AI workloads.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The firm noted that the top 10 automotive Tier 1 suppliers consumed about 54% of total DRAM (Dynamic Random Access Memory) in 2025, led by giants like BYD (BYDDF), Tesla (TSLA), and Aptiv (APTV). Yet autos account for under 10% of the global DRAM market, leaving the sector at a disadvantage as chipmakers prioritize higher-margin clients in cloud computing and AI.

Tight Parts Supply Could Raise Auto Prices

DRAM is widely used for vehicle infotainment, advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS), and centralized electronic architectures. Its prices have skyrocketed lately, stripping carmakers of bargaining leverage. Wells Fargo noted that DDR5 spot prices have surged over eight times and DDR4 more than sixteen times above 2024 averages.

Analysts estimate current DRAM content at $50 to $110 per vehicle, cautioning that these hikes could deliver a hefty cost hit to the auto sector in 2026. They noted that global DRAM demand is expected to climb by about 26% next year, outpacing supply growth of roughly 21%.

Micron (MU) remains the largest supplier to the auto sector, along with Samsung (SSNLF) and SK Hynix. These chipmakers are allocating greater time and resources to build the faster growing and more profitable data center markets than the auto sector.

Consequently, automakers and suppliers may face steeper prices to secure supply, a risk Wells Fargo warns could squeeze margins and disrupt production schedules. Analysts added that premium vehicle segments and EV makers appear most vulnerable due to their elevated memory usage per vehicle.

Wells Fargo already sees emerging signs of panic buying similar to that of the 2021 chip crisis, warning that failure to absorb higher costs could trigger production halts.

Tesla or BYD: Which Is the Better Auto Stock?

A tightening memory market could raise auto costs and pressure margins, particularly for memory-heavy EVs like Tesla and BYD.

We used the TipRanks Stock Comparison Tool to determine which stock between Tesla and BYD is currently favored by analysts. Notably, BYDDF stock has a "Strong Buy," while Tesla has a "Hold" consensus, suggesting analysts remain highly optimistic about BYD's long-term prospects.
data.author: Sheryl Sheth

data.title: Why Stifel Turns More Bullish on Ondas Stock (ONDS) After Investor Day Update
data.description:
Ondas Holdings (ONDS) drew fresh support from Wall Street after hosting a recent virtual investor day, where the company laid out its plans for its drone and autonomous systems business. The company raised its 2026 revenue target to $170 million–$180 million, citing a growing pipeline and improving visibility. Following the event, top Stifel analyst Jonathan Siegmann raised his price target on Ondas to $18 from $17 and kept a Buy rating on the stock.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Investor Day Strengthens the Bull Case

Siegmann said the investor day increased his confidence in Ondas' long-term story, especially its position in the military drone market. He noted that the company's revenue and growth targets were well above his earlier estimates and showed clearer progress across the business.

He added that the presentation also gave him a better understanding of how Ondas stands out in the defense drone space, where demand continues to rise.

At the same time, Siegmann acknowledged that Ondas is still early in building its business. Even so, he pointed to the speed of recent progress as a positive sign. Over the past six months, the company has raised about $1.8 billion, acquired new technology, partnered with major contract manufacturers, and strengthened its leadership team.

Overall, Siegmann believes these steps are helping Ondas move faster and build a stronger base for growth. As a result, he expects 2026 to be an important year for the company and sees potential for the stock to perform well as execution continues.

Is Ondas Holdings a Good Stock to Buy?  

Currently, Wall Street has a Strong Buy consensus rating on Ondas stock based on eight unanimous Buy recommendations. The average ONDS stock price target of $16.63 indicates about 26.66% upside potential.
data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: Is VTI a Good ETF? A Quick Snapshot for Investors —1/21/25
data.description:

The fund offers broad exposure to thousands of companies across large, mid, and small caps, giving investors instant diversification. With a low expense ratio of just 0.03%, VTI aims to match overall market performance, not outperform it—making it a simple, low-cost option for investors who want diversified U.S. equity exposure without choosing individual stocks.

data.title: Airbus to Use "Squatting" Chinese Robots to Build New Aircraft Following UBTech Deal
data.description:
Aviation manufacturer Airbus (EADSY) has sealed a deal with Chinese firm UBTech to use its "squatting and lifting" humanoid robots to build new planes.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Early Testing Stage

UBTech said Airbus has bought its latest industrial humanoid robot, the Walker S2, and that the "two parties will jointly expand the application of humanoid robots in aerospace manufacturing scenarios in the future."

Airbus said in a statement that the cooperation with UBTech was ‍in the early concept-testing stage.

The move follows a similar partnership UBTech formed last year with U.S. semiconductor maker Texas Instruments (TXN). It also purchased the Walker S2, and is currently reportedly deploying and debugging it for application on its production line.

UBTech said that the Walker S2 has been specifically engineered for industrial tasks, with a height of 1.76m, 15kg payload capacity, human vision perception and 24/7 operation enabled by an autonomous battery-swapping system. It can also carry out deep squats and stoop lifting.

The company also boasts a Panda robot, which can paint and greet people with a friendly smile at exhibitions and events.

UBTech said total orders for its humanoid robots hit 1.4 billion yuan, about $200 million, in 2025, while production capacity for its industrial humanoid robots is expected to exceed 10,000 units in 2026.

China Takes the Lead

Humanoid robots are built and designed to look like and move like human beings. They are powered by AI algorithms and complex hardware like semiconductors. They could be used in a whole range of settings from the battlefield, to the factory floor, in hospital surgeries or dusting away the cobwebs in your living room.

According to new data from Omdia, Chinese humanoid robot makers dominated the market in 2025, with five firms in the top 10.
data.author: David Craik

data.title: Nvidia, Palantir and Dell Stocks Rise on Deal to Build AI Data Centers across EMEA
data.description:
Shares in tech firms Nvidia (NVDA), Palantir (PLTR) and Dell (DELL) edged higher early Wednesday after UK-based Sovereign AI picked them and professional services firm Accenture (ACN) to build AI data centers across Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA).

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Palantir and Accenture Spearhead Sovereign Cloud Project

The partnership is being spearheaded by Palantir, which will lead the software front while Accenture helps with operational control, including sales and engineering support. Specifically, Palantir will use its Chain Reaction operating system to build and deploy the AI infrastructure.

To achieve this, the Dell AI Factory with Nvidia — Dell's end-to-end stack of hardware, software and services — will function as the "secure, high-performance backbone" for the project. The stack is powered by Nvidia's AI capabilities such as the Blackwell graphics processing unit and is specifically tailored for massive AI workloads

Sovereign AI noted that the goal is to "deliver a resilient sovereign AI foundation for commercial and government sectors" across EMEA.

The Sovereign Cloud Race Heats Up

Sovereign AI refers to a type of cloud infrastructure that maintains data within a specific country or region in terms of physical location, legal frameworks, and management. 

"This collaboration with Accenture and Palantir will help accelerate the development and deployment of [Palantir's] Dell AI Factory- and NVIDIA-powered sovereign-grade AI capabilities, supporting economic security and digital resiliency across EMEA, with plans to expand into Asia Pacific," Sovereign AI explained in a statement.

The deal builds on an existing partnership between Palantir and Accenture, which together formed the Accenture Palantir Business Group in December. Palantir named Accenture its global partner for enterprise transformation as part of the deal.

Meanwhile, the new project comes as governments and industries across the world continue to consider sovereign AI as critical. Last week, Amazon Web Services (AMZN) launched the European Sovereign Cloud in Germany as its first sovereign cloud service.

data.title: Netflix Stock (NFLX) Faces Divided Views from Morgan Stanley and Goldman After Q4 Results
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) stock remains in focus after missing analyst estimates for Q2 after its latest earnings report, which showed steady growth but also raised new questions around strategy and valuation. Following the release, some analysts remain bullish on Netflix's long-term earnings power, while others are urging patience as investors wait for more clarity.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Q4 Earnings Snapshot

Netflix reported a solid quarter, with revenue, operating profit, and free cash flow all coming in strong. The company reported fourth-quarter revenue of $12.1 billion, modestly above Wall Street expectations of $11.97 billion, while adjusted earnings came in at $0.56 per share, also slightly ahead of forecasts of $0.55 per share.

Meanwhile, the company also said advertising revenue more than doubled last year and is expected to grow further in 2026. However, Netflix confirmed it has paused share buybacks as it prepares for its proposed all-cash deal for Warner Bros. Discovery's (WBD) streaming and studio assets.

Bullish View: Morgan Stanley Sees Long-Term EPS Growth

Morgan Stanley analyst Benjamin Swinburne kept an Overweight rating on Netflix but lowered his price target to $110 from $120. Swinburne said his positive view is based on Netflix's ability to grow adjusted earnings by more than 20% per year through 2028, driven by scale, improving margins, and strong returns on content spending. He also highlighted Netflix's fast-growing ad business, which grew more than 100% year over year in 2025 and is expected to reach over $3 billion in revenue in 2026.

On the Warner Bros deal, Swinburne said risks appear largely priced in, noting that Netflix's forward valuation has already come down sharply. He added that if the deal closes, it could improve earnings and boost returns on content over time.

Cautious View: Goldman Sachs Wants More Clarity

Goldman Sachs analyst Eric Sheridan lowered his price target to $100 from $112 and kept a Neutral rating.

Sheridan acknowledged Netflix's strong Q4 results and said the company could still deliver solid revenue growth and improving margins in 2026. However, he said investors may hold back until there is more clarity on the cost, structure, and impact of the WBD deal.

He added that while the deal could help Netflix grow and compete, the stock may stay under pressure until investors have a clearer view on long-term returns and how cash will be used.

Is NFLX Stock a Good Buy Now?  

Turning to Wall Street, analysts have a Moderate Buy consensus rating on NFLX stock based on 26 Buys, nine Holds, and two Sells assigned in the past three months, as indicated by the graphic below. Furthermore, the average NFLX price target of $124.63 per share implies 43% upside potential.
data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: OpenAI Launches Chatbot Ads to Diversify Revenue, Easing Subscription Reliance
data.description:
ChatGPT maker OpenAI has started offering its new chatbot ads to select advertisers in a bid to increase revenues and reduce reliance on subscriptions, according to The Information. The artificial intelligence (AI) startup is asking advertisers to commit less than $1 million each during a several-week trial, with ads going live in February.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The step also aims to boost ad revenue and help offset the significant costs of technology development. OpenAI's ad model differs from the rest, as it is charging advertisers based on ad views instead of the popular click-based method.

OpenAI's Strategic Pivot to Ads

OpenAI currently does not offer any ad-buying tools for advertisers, but it is developing this feature to draw in more advertisers and improve future ad deployment. The company faces intense pressure to scale revenues amid heavy spending on data centers and preparations for a potential initial public offering (IPO). Last week, it announced ad testing in ChatGPT's free tier for select U.S. users.

Tech giant Microsoft (MSFT) is one of OpenAI's largest investors. Yesterday, OpenAI disclosed a blockbuster annualized revenue run rate exceeding $20 billion for 2025, up from $6 billion in 2024. OpenAI's computing capacity has also surged to about 1.9 gigawatts from 0.6 gigawatts a year earlier.

These figures illustrate how AI usage growth is aligning with increased spending on computing power. OpenAI also noted record highs in weekly and daily user activity, suggesting AI tools are increasingly integrating into daily work and consumer habits.

Which Is the Best ChatGPT Stock?

We used the TipRanks Stock Comparison Tool to determine which ChatGPT stock is currently preferred by analysts.

Wall Street has assigned a "Strong Buy" consensus rating to Baidu (BIDU), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft, Alphabet (GOOGL), and Nvidia (NVDA) stocks, with NVDA shares offering the highest upside potential over the next twelve months.
data.author: Sheryl Sheth

data.title: Markets Stay Calm as Investors Bet Trump Will Back Down on Tariffs
data.description:
Investors are trying to stay calm amid new tensions between the U.S. and Europe. President Trump said several European countries could face new tariffs within weeks. The warning is tied to U.S. efforts to buy Greenland, which is owned by Denmark and not for sale.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

At first glance, markets look uneasy but not panicked. The VIX volatility index is up 27% over the past five days. That is the highest level since April last year, when Trump announced global tariffs.

Why Investors Are Not Panicking Yet

However, market behavior shows that many investors are not fully buying into the threat. That view is shaped by past-experience. In April last year, markets plunged after Trump's Liberation Day tariff speech. Yet many of those tariffs were delayed within days. As a result, traders coined the phrase Trump Always Chickens Out, known as the TACO trade. Since then, investors have often faded tariff headlines rather than chase fear.

Jim Reid of Deutsche Bank (DB) summed it up clearly. He told clients that the market has "been burnt before by overreacting to tariff threats." He also noted that there is still "room for bigger moves" if policy risk grows.

There is also a legal factor at play. Reid pointed to a pending Supreme Court ruling on whether earlier tariffs were legal. He said this could limit Trump's ability to act, although the timing remains unclear.

Meanwhile, bond markets suggest caution rather than alarm. UBS (UBS) chief economist Paul Donovan said investors are watching U.S. bonds closely. He added that bond traders "do not seem to be taking the threats too seriously."

When Calm Becomes a Risk

That calm may explain why betting markets remain skeptical. On Polymarket, only 17% of bettors expect all threatened tariffs to start on Feb. 1. Denmark has the highest odds at 40%, with France close behind at 38%. Political pressure also matters. Polls show Trump's approval ratings have slipped. Only 17% of voters support efforts to acquire Greenland, according to a Reuters Ipsos survey.

Still, there is a risk in assuming history will repeat itself. Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen warned about this dynamic last year. He said, "As markets discount the tariffs and perform strongly, that's actually making the higher tariffs more likely."
data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: Stock Market News Today, 1/21/26 – U.S. Stock Futures Rise after S&P 500, Dow's Largest Drop Since October
data.description:
U.S. stock futures edged higher early Wednesday after the S&P 500 and the 30-stock Dow recorded their largest single-day drops since October 10. Traders are reacting to President Donald Trump's threat to escalate tariffs over his demand to acquire Greenland.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Futures on the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), the S&P 500 (SPX), and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) were up 0.33%, 0.34%, and 0.21%, respectively, at 3:20 a.m. EST on January 21.

During Tuesday's regular trading session, the three major indexes closed sharply lower, with technology stocks leading the selloff. The Dow declined by 1.76%, the S&P 500 fell by 2.06%, and the Nasdaq Composite dropped 2.39%.

After the markets closed, Netflix (NFLX) stock fell more than 5%, despite beating sales and earnings expectations for Q4FY25 and reporting a solid surge in global paid subscribers to 325 million. Moreover, the streaming giant revised its bid for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) to an all-cash offer, fending off competition from Paramount Skydance (PSKY).

Looking forward, traders are focused on Trump's speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos today, where he will discuss the Greenland issue. Additionally, economic reports on housing starts, MBA mortgage applications, building permits, and pending home sales will be released today.

On the earnings front, companies reporting today include Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Halliburton (HAL), and Charles Schwab (SCHW).

Notably, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was down, floating near 4.26%. WTI crude oil futures were trending lower, hovering near $59.63 per barrel as of the last check. Additionally, the Gold Spot U.S. dollar price increased to nearly $4,855 per ounce on Wednesday.

Elsewhere, European indexes opened lower today as traders anticipate Trump's speech in front of world leaders at Davos.

Asia-Pacific Markets Mixed

Asia-Pacific markets traded mixed on January 21 amid escalated geopolitical tensions involving Iran and Greenland.

Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index rose 0.36%. In China, the Shanghai Composite slipped 0.08%, while the Shenzhen Component gained 0.80%. Meanwhile, Japan's Nikkei dropped 0.41%, and the Topix declined 0.99%.

data.author: Sheryl Sheth
